replies.author: @SoleProxy
replies.text:
​@ChaptermasterPedroKantor-kv5ywI'm Finnish and it feels like you're casting blame when you don't even know what happened.

Finland wasn't a formal treaty ally like the Tripartite Pact between Germany, Italy and Japan. We fought the Winter War alone and any support from the Allies like Britain was very slow and unreliable though Sweden was more useful. We lost that war and in the end lost land and 400,000 Finns were displaced. Our government and military did predict that the Soviets would attack us again and Germany was our best option for a strong ally against the USSR. We occupied lands and participated in the siege because that's what you do in war, but we never got to keep any of that because we still lost. We even had to chase the German army out of our lands through the north which is where Sisus protagonist is looking for gold, uncaring about the war or politics. The Germans notice he has gold and try to take it from him. Then he goes on a John Wick-like rampage against them.

We absolutely do not hide away or whitewash the fact we co-operated with Germany during the Continuation War. We have old documentaries talking about it on television too. Those two wars are a big part of our young nation which when combined with the fact that Russia is an authoritarian nation now invading Ukraine in an attempt to "free" people has galvanized us even further against them, and the fact that we used to be under them in the first place before we gained our independence.

@kborton99 Here is the full speech from the book, and description. The movie did it massive justice. The books even go as far as to compare him to the Valar gods, and the only human to be compared like that in the book.
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
But at that same moment there was a flash, as if lightning had sprung from the earth beneath the City. For a searing second it stood dazzling far off in black and white, its topmost tower like a glittering needle: and then as the darkness closed again there came rolling over the fields a great boom.

At that sound the bent shape of the king sprang suddenly erect. Tall and proud he seemed again; and rising in his stirrups he cried in a loud voice, more clear than any there had ever heard a mortal man achieve before: Arise, arise, Riders of Théoden! Fell deeds awake: fire and slaughter! spear shall be shaken, shield be splintered, a sword-day, a red day, ere the sun rises! Ride now, ride now! Ride to Gondor! With that he seized a great horn from Guthláf his banner-bearer, and he blew such a blast upon it that it burst asunder. And straightway all the horns in the host were lifted up in music, and the blowing of the horns of Rohan in that hour was like a storm upon the plain and a thunder in the mountains. Ride now, ride now! Ride to Gondor! 

Suddenly the king cried to Snowmane and the horse sprang away. Behind him his banner blew in the wind, white horse upon a field of green, but he outpaced it. After him thundered the knights of his house, but he was ever before them. Éomer rode there, the white horsetail on his helm floating in his speed, and the front of the first éored roared like a breaker foaming to the shore, but Théoden could not be overtaken. Fey he seemed, or the battle-fury of his fathers ran like new fire in his veins, and he was borne up on Snowmane like a god of old, even as Oromë the Great in the battle of the Valar when the world was young. His golden shield was uncovered, and lo! it shone like an image of the Sun, and the grass flamed into green about the white feet of his steed. 

For morning came, morning and a wind from the sea; and the darkness was removed, and the hosts of Mordor wailed, and terror took them, and they fled, and died, and the hoofs of wrath rode over them. And then all the host of Rohan burst into song, and they sang as they slew, for the joy of battle was on them, and the sound of their singing that was fair and terrible came even to the City.

replies.author: @Simon-xc5oy
replies.text: @ukaszwrobel5968 Yep. And every year you waited eagerly for Christmas as you knew the next film was coming out. For the first film, in the six months leading up to it I was re reading the books for the millionth time, and my then girlfriend could not understand my obsession. But she asked me and I explained some of the plot and back story. Every few days she asked more questions, till in the end, one night in bed after the usual romping she asked me to read some to her. I was about to get into the mines of Moria so that was where I started. I was almost hoarse an hour or so later, she would not let me stop reading and wanted to know what happened. It became a thing every night more or less I had to read the book to her. Till we got to see the film. When we came out I after it I was subdued and she was raving about how great it was, and why was I not more enthused. And I had to explain to her that I had been waiting to see them make this book into a film, since I first had it read to me when I was about five years old. And to finally see it, and see it done right and the characters brought to life so well, I was overcome with so many emotions. It was the first time since the original Star Wars films a movie had effected me so much on so many levels. And the last....Since these three films nothing has ever come close again.

@Simon-xc5oy When i was 8 or 9, thats almost 60 years ago, my father bought a magazine, here in Sweden, which was called the "Jules Verne Magazine". In that mag, i found  an article about an exiting book called "The Hobbit", and i begged my father to buy that book. 
It was my night lecture for several years, until i found the books of The Lord of The Rings trilogy.  
I read that, over and over. 
I was mad when a friend of mine lost one of my books on a vacation to Paris...  i still remember how upset i was.
But i read the books, and thought to my self: "-Its soo  sad, because this will NEVER be made into a real movie..."
Then one day, it was announced that an animated movie had been made...!  
It was just of the first book in the trilogy, and it was an awful quality, and had lost soo much of the lore and feeling....
There was never a follow-up movie of the second book....  This was in my late teens, and i still loved LOTR.
The experience never left me.
Then now, and at my age of 68,  already 25 years after the trilogy was complete.... i am still young and looking into the amazing adventure!!!

author: @loragunning5394
text:
I first read TLOTR trilogy when I was 14 yrs old, back in 1968.  It completely changed my life, forever.  I read it non-stop, over and over, back to back, for the next 4 years.  In my own head, I became a 10th member of The Fellowship, a phantom walking alongside each and every one of them all the way thru the entire story.  In the decades of my life that have passed since then, I have returned to reading the trilogy at least every couple of years and it has NEVER lost it's magic for me.  When I learned that there were finally going to be movies made of the story, with live actors, my immediate reaction was one of intrepidation.  I had of course very strong internal visuals of each character by then, what each person looked like, how they sounded when they talked, what the world of Middle Earth looked like.  And I knew each and every person who had read the stories had their own internal visuals, each as individual as the persons themselves.  How could a film be made that would possibly satisfy all these millions of die-hard, hard core Tolkien fans such as myself without hugely disappointing most of them?  So many fears, anxieties!  Would the film honor the canon of the books, the lore that Tolkien so very carefully built over the decades it took him to write these stories?  The languages, the history?  I was really very torn about whether or not to see the first film at all, so afraid that my own internal visions of Tolkien's created world, would be totally and forever shattered by somebody else's visions.  

But of course, in the end, I did go see the first film.  Opening day, no less.  And while yeah, Peter Jackson's take on the story was different than mine in small ways, and yeah I had some issues with casting choices, and yeah some of the landscapes and scenery didn't match what I had been married to for over 30 years, but by the time the film ended, I knew that it was going to be OK for me.  It was close enough to my own internal visions that it worked, and worked very well, for me.  And I wept, literally, with gratitude, and a feeling of release from high anxiety, as I left the theater and walked home afterwards.  Someone else finally got it, that deep reverence of a work of fiction so well written as to be a timeless classic, and respected it.  

I have watched the 3 films almost yearly since then, in a non-stop marathon, on Christmas Day.  I still read the books every couple of years.  After 3 tries, I finally made it all the way through The Simarillion.  It's a tough slog, but it has the most beautiful creation story I have ever read.

My own favorite scene, in both the books and the movies, is the charge of the Rohirrim on the plain of The Pellinor Fields.  In the books, Minas Tirith is in flames, under siege, and Pippin and Gandalf have just witnessed Denethor's final madness and ensuing death.  Dawn is upon them as they make their way back into the city from The Houses of the Dead, when they both hear horns blowing in the distance.  "Rohan had come at last."  Peter Jackson filmed this so perfectly.  The scene of Gandalf and Pippin on the battlements high up in Minas Tirith, dawn breaking in the sky behind them, facing down The Witch King (never happens in the books, BTW), then faint horns in the background.  Cut to Theodan and Eomer stopping on a rise to view the battle scene before them at the gates of the city, the entire Rohirrim army coming up behind them, the sun rising behind them.  Gives me chills and brings tears to my eyes EVERY SINGLE TIME, despite having seen it at least 15 times over the past 25 years.  The saving of Helm's Deep is a great moment, I grant you that, but for me at least, it can't hold a candle to the charge of the Rohirrim across the fields of the Pellinor.

author: @burt2800
text:
The main thing that makes The Lord of the Rings so special is the subtle sadness and sense of loss that underlies the world. The elves, bearers of eternal wisdom and witnesses of the very creation of the world, are leaving. Kingdoms are old and decaying. We are lesser sons of greater sires. And the  forces of darkness are ever lurking and ready to pounce.
And yet it's about hope. Hope that the courage of men will lead the world to a new dawn. That the sun will rise again. Not to give us paradise, but another chance to fight, another chance to fight and die. And we would not have it any other way. Again and again and again unto the end of time, so that our home, our precious Shire, can live on. So that we can have a place to have a pint, and put our feet up before a cozy fire.

Magic itself is leaving middle earth in a way. But like you said its also chalk full of hope, hope for a better future if you're willing to fight for it. replies.text:
I had an entire semester class dedicated to the study of Tolkien and his literature. We read his letters, his poems, his essays, and of course, the LoTR. 

One of the questions we continually asked as a class was "how did Tolkien think of Hope?" 

We never managed to arrive at a straight conclusion, there is simply so much nuance, depth, soul and truth in his work. It's not like definitions or ideas weren't proposed, but none felt capable of grasping it.

What I took away from that is that if you want to understand Hope, you have to read the LoTR again, and again, and again.

replies.author: @TheEarlofBronze1
replies.text:
​@burt2800If i had to put money on a "central" scene that sort of summarized or encapsulated the entirety of LoTR it would be the scene when Frodo and Sam are in Mordor and Sam looks up and briefly, just briefly, sees a star beyond shining pure and bright beyond the corrupting darkness of Mordor.

Its a lance of pure goodness and beauty that the darkness can never touch, and it finds its way into a needy heart in the darkest of moments.

It perfectly marries Tolkiens desire to write a fable, with his desire to "ennoble" the small and little folk if the world (he mentions this idea in a letter), and hope and beauty of his Christian faith in a Good (God) greater than any darkness.

replies.text:
Some jokes from Extended Edition were a bit unnecessary, especially adaptation-wise (he's much more serious and majestic in the books). But most of Gimli's comedic scenes are tied to his character development (ex.: from "Nobody tosses a dwarf" to "Toss me") and the lore importance behind his relationship with a Elf. They helped his ultimate line "Aye, I could do that" sounds genuinely powerful and sincere.

 I wouldnt call these humoristic scenes in Helm's Deep comedy or a joke though. "Lighthearted" would be a more precise word. The kill counting is straigth from the books. They are top warriors from their race and thats their way to relieve themselves from the stress of the battle. It doesn't break the immersion or the seriousness of the situation.

author: @eoin401
text:
Hi guys, original trilogy crew member here. I was on 2nd unit during the Helms Deep sequences, it was some of the hardest work any of us had ever done.
So many difficulties to overcome, incredible crew camaraderie made it possible.
Glad you guys loved it so much ❤
Arohanui from Aotearoa

replies.author: @BarelyCynical
replies.text: God Bless you Arohanui from Aotearoa! You were part of one of the most legendary film sequences ever created. What was your role on the 2nd Unit? What VERY few fans even know is a man VERY few have ever even heard of: John Mahaffie. It was HIM, and NOT Peter Jackson who was the Director of the 2nd Unit responsible for filming the Battle Of Helm’s Deep over the course of a brutal 4-month, 14 hour a “day”.. (night!), film shoot. John Mahaffie has received nearly ZERO credit for his insane work, a masterpiece 99.9% of fans have always attributed solely to Director Peter Jackson.. because why wouldn’t they if never told of the unsung hero who ACTUALLY directed this battle. Can YOU please speak on him a little for us?? 🙏

replies.author: @oodaks
replies.text:
Thank you for your service 🫡
It was such a buzz in Aotearoa New Zealand when the filming was going on. I still have a habit of pointing out LOTR filming locations about the motu, even the ones that I'm only kinda sure were 😆
Chur 🤙🇳🇿

replies.author: @nelsonthegentleman2756
replies.text: Thank you for this masterpiece

replies.author: @sir_lanian
replies.text: "Arohanui" is Te Rio Maori for 'Much Love'. 'Aoteoroa' is also Te Rio Maori for 'New Zealand'. I should know. I'm a kiwi as well.

replies.author: @eoin401
replies.text:
@B@BarelyCynicalu are correct. John was a really lovely guy, quite short with a powerful aura. He was under incredible pressure but kept cool 99% of the time.
His first AD Dave knew him well and they worked well together. 
Mahhafie was chosen because Pete wouldn’t trust anybody else to do it, Mahhafies vision was enormous and matched Peters in this way.
He was also a man of faith, but I never really heard him say anything about it, he kept it private and personal.

An absolutely lovely guy, and he went on to do a lot more big projects after the success of the rings, those who knew - knew.
Great question by the way, I comment in alot of reaction videos because the fandom is so strong and it fills me with pride, but nobody has ever asked about Mahhafie before.
Those night shoots were brutal and broke some of us, it took an absolute will of steel within directorial leadership to bring us through it.

author: @Krushner20
text:
Just been to see all three again 25 years after I went to see the Fellowship aged 13

Just as good now as it was back then.  

In fact, even better.  Because this time I had the appreciation of just how good this trilogy was.  And it was also the extended editions.

There will never be one anywhere near as good.  We are fortunate. 

Those of us who got to see the films on the big screen twice are a lucky generation indeed.  

I went alone this time.  And that made it even better still.  An experience I will cherish now aged 38 until the day I die

What a great video and fitting tribute to the best trilogy of all time

replies.author: @TangSooTerp
replies.text: The original Star Wars trilogy did that for me. I thoroughly enjoyed the LOTR trilogy, and think it was some phenomenal filmmaking. But I grew up on Star Wars, and it introduced me to science fiction, Greek mythology, and Eastern martial arts, to name a few things. It will always be special to me, and ranks in my mind as the best trilogy ever. But LOTR was so good, it has earned a high place as well. 😊

replies.author: @deniswauchope3788
replies.text: @TangSooTerp I'm like you but the other way around. I'm old now, but have loved science fiction since my boyhood back in the '50s. I'd seen so many "Sci-Fi" films that were just horror films, or creature features, but not actually Sci-Fi, that I was sick of being disappointed at the movies. My friends dragged me along to see Star Wars in 1977 though- and tears were running down my face in pure awe and joy before the opening credits had finished! So yes, that Star Wars film was incredible for me... but. I'd read LOTR about 50 times before Peter Jackson had even started to film the trilogy, and (similarly to Star Wars) I was greatly in doubt that he'd done it with reverence for the text (which I could quote pages of;) so though I was reluctant I went to see The Fellowship of the Ring... and this struck me even harder than Star Wars, it was as close to perfect as anyone could achieve. So though I loved both films, and I'm sad that the original Star Wars film that I saw can't be viewed any more (since Lucas went and changed it, the jerk,) for me LOTR will always be at the pinnacle of film theater.

author: @taliablack4080
text:
My new best friend I met at college had never heard of Lord of the Rings. 

It wasn't that she had never read the books or sat down to watch the movies, she had never heard of Lord of the Rings period. So naturally in the interest of our ongoing friendship I invited her over every Friday for three weeks to watch them with me. We just finished Return of the King last week and we sat in complete silence for about ten minutes after the movie ended before exploding into conversation that continued for almost two hours. 

Since then she has ordered second-hand copies of the books and is bombing my texts with all the new lore she has been unpacking from various media essays. Never let these movies be forgotten.

text: My favorite element of the Lord of the Rings that separates it from many other stories will forever be the fact that the big climactic moments are massive battles between armies, and the fact that the focus really is on the armies, rather than what many many stories have done since time immemorial (not just modern movies) which is a 1 on 1 or small group fight between the hero(es) and villain(s). Agreed.   Gandalfs arrival at helms deep is great don’t get me wrong.  But the ride of the rohirrim at Pelennor gives me actual goosebumps.  The scale is bigger the music hits harder, and there is a theme difference.  

At helms deep Theoden was making a last stand in defense and the arrival of Gandalf and eomer are like the relief after hope was lost and a feeling all is saved.  At Pelennor there is this theme of them going against something insurmountable but they are going to charge anyway.  The difference is at helms deep they are defending and don’t really have a choice.  There is no where to retreat.  At Pelennor theoden and Rohan don’t have to be there.  They are not defending they are attacking.

replies.author: @peterk2735
replies.text:
That’s what’s missing from modern fantasy, everything is this morally ambiguous, nihilistic slop. Tolkien’s generation experienced humanity at its lowest point during WW1. Mustard gas, cholera, mass charges against machine guns, brutal trench warfare etc, then they had to send their children to die in WW2. 

So after the war, writers like Tolkien gave us books that were meant to teach us how basic human values like compassion, honesty, friendship, bravery, etc could overcome tyranny and evil. They thought it would help us become better people and avoid their mistakes, but these days I feel like we’ve totally let them down 😢

replies.text: @JonCom3dy In the movie, Theoden goes to Helm's Deep against the advice of Gandalf. In the book, Gandalf leaves Edoras with the king's army to meet the enemy at the fords of Isen, but when they learn that the fords have fallen, he advises Theoden to go to Helm's Deep while he gathers the remnants of the westfold army (the one that defended the fords of Isen) and comes back at dawn with this unexpected reinforcement. Also in the book, no elves came (except Legolas), Eomer was not banned and instead follows the king from Edoras to Helm's Deep, Eowin was not at Helm's Deep, the people of Edoras are sent to Dunharrow and not to Helm's Deep, there is no skirmish against the king's army and wargs raiders before Helm's Deep, there are many Dunmen fighting alongside the Huruk-hai at Helm's Deep, Gimli ends up fighting inside the caves after the wall is blown up...

text:
I believe that the cavalry charge at Helm's Deep was inspired by the Siege of Vienna in 1683, when 3,000 Polish Hussars led the largest cavalry force in history down Kahlenberg Mountain to utterly smash the Ottoman forces and lift the siege. This battle is literally and figuratively known as 'the battle that saved Europe', another in a long line of famous battles in European history going back as far as the Greco-Persian wars, where heavily outnumbered and hopeless European forces fight back the hordes of the east in the most heroic and iconic struggles in our history.
I watch the extended trilogy every 6 months or so, religiously and since I first watched the Fellowship in the cinema when it first released. Absolutely epic cinema in all its glory.

replies.author: @Javier-u8v1e
replies.text:
Tolkien for this part of the book was inspired by the legend of Santiago the Apostle during the Spanish reconquest on "la Batalla de Clavijo"

The Christian troops advanced, but when they reached Nájera and Albelda, they were surrounded by a large number of Muslim troops under the command of Abd al-Rahman II. 
The Christians took refuge in the castle of Clavijo and there, King Ramiro had a premonitory dream. 
He dreamed that Santiago Apóstol appeared to him and told him that he would be by his side during the battle that would take place the next day and that his troops would rise victorious. According to the chronicles of the twelfth century, on May 23, 844, the so-called Battle of Clavijo took place. When the Christian hosts were being massacred by the Saracens, Santiago Apóstol miraculously appeared riding a white steed and attacked the Muslims. 
The Christians, encouraged, rushed after him and managed to defeat their opponents. Santiago Apostle, who was given the nickname "Santiago Matamoros" became a symbol of the struggle against Islam.

replies.text:
I will argue that it's not the battle of Helms Deep that was inspird by the second ottoman siege of vienna but the ride of the Rohirrim to the battle of Minas Thirith / the Siege of Minas Tirith.
Reasons being: In the second ottman siege of vienna, the relief army (of which the polish troops of polish King Jan Sobiesky made up roughly 1/3rd of the troops) marched through the night over a little mountain (The Kahlenberg) which is located to the north west of the city. They marched through the thick forrest lead by locals. Slow because the infantery had to keep up, and without break.  This was the only direction the ottomans did not expect anyone to come from, because they deemed the mountain and the forrest covering it not suitable for cavalry and heavy weapons to get through and attack them. 
They reached the "edge of the battlefield at dawn, went into formation and began their attack. (the famous polish troops made up the right flank if i remember correct). And while that happened, the besieged realized what was happening, and attacked outwards.
And now compare that with the ride of the Rohirrim: They took paths through thick forrest guided by Ghan bury Ghan and his people (locals) they came from the norht west to the battlefieeld from a direction the Orks deemed to be safe from
They reached the battlefield at dawn (remember the rooster crying before everybody heard their horns).
And they did not make breaks on their march, did not rest, but had moved through the night and the only little rest they had was to get into formation and start their attack in a coordinated way.
The biggest difference between the two is that the Rohirrim were only Rohirrim and their force did not consist of at least 4 different nationalities (what counts as nationalities today) and that the leader of the relief army did not spearhead the attack in the same way Theoden did (because in reality, snowmane did not exist)

replies.author: @Skaldzerker
replies.text:
Though they are similar in the structure of events, the Battle of Minas Tirith was definitely inspired by the Siege of Vienna, not the Battle of Helm's Deep. Start with the fact that Vienna and Minas Tirith are both capital cities, while Helm's Deep is just a castle. Even just the fact that the relieving cavalry force was larger at the Ride of the Rohirrim points explicitly to this and fits the scale of the Siege of Vienna much more appropriately, as the Polish Winged Hussars were the 3,000 strong vanguard of a 18-20k horse assault. Geographically speaking, the Uruk-hai came to Helm's Deep from the north, while the hosts of Mordor attacked Minas Tirith from the east/southeast, which is the same direction Vienna was marched upon by the Ottomans.

On the level of historical importance, as well, the Battle of Helm's Deep was not nearly as significant for the entirety Middle Earth as the Battle of Minas Tirith. The Battle of Minas Tirith (Siege of Vienna) was the breaking of the host of Sauron (army of the Ottomans), and neither Mordor nor the Ottomans threatened Middle Earth (Europe) so significantly ever again.

Also, regarding the important of the Battle of Helm's Deep, outside of the involvement of key characters like Gandalf and Aragorn, it didn't really matter if Rohan won or lost. Don't forget that in the books, the host of Mordor never set foot in Minas Tirith, as the Men of Gondor were holding them easily at the gate. They did not need the assistance of Rohan to win the battle. The Ride of the Rohirrim was more symbolic of the joining of humans against evil than a military necessity. In the movie, Gondor (and especially Denethor) was specifically made weaker to manufacture tension. You can make the argument that Rohan would be needed to push Mordor back and destroy Sauron, but that's another discussion.

Hot take: I never was too keen on that detail. Not just because it wasn't in the books. Lothlórien was literally days away from Rohan... word of the invasion had barely reached Théoden and hadn't even reached Éomer, yet somehow Celeborn and Galadriel AND Elrond (who was even FURTHER away) knew about it and were able to dispatch a whole army to Helm's Deep... an army that reached Helm's Deep before the Uruk-Hai, who were already in Rohan? And how did they know the Rohirrim had retreated to Helm's Deep? And why is it that, after the retreat to the keep, all the Elves vanished? Were they all killed? As Drinker says: "Don't know -- just go with it."

It just felt shoehorned in to give the Elves more screentime.

That's not to say I don't like the Battle of Helm's Deep. I just feel the elven army element is way too contrived.

I hope you make this a series. Exploring different iconic scenes in cinema history and what made them so deserving of praise. 

For me, I think Luke's fight with Vader and the Emperor is my contribution. 

The cinematography, the soundtrack swelling to perfection, the dialogue, and then the character dynamics. It's executed flawlessly, and I think it marks the end of the film MORE than the Death Star exploding. 

When Luke loses control to defend his sister, striking Vader until he falls and is defenceless- Mm! Perfection. Teetering on the edge of the abyss, but calming himself and rejecting it in totality, putting his body on the line to protect his soul, allowing his father to redeem himself and fulfill the prophecy (a hindsight addition of course, but a satisfying one). 

Just excellence.
